title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Inclusion of Arts in the STEM Curriculum (STEAM)

texts.A: The push to turn STEM into STEAM by adding 'Arts' is a well-meaning but misguided attempt to soften subjects that are naturally rigorous. Science and mathematics require a level of precision and objective truth that is fundamentally different from the subjective nature of artistic expression. [37]By diluting the curriculum, we risk falling behind in the global race for technological innovation.[/37] The arts are vital to a civilized society, but they should be pursued as a complement to a technical education, not integrated into it in a way that distracts from core competencies.
texts.B: The most significant breakthroughs in history have come from individuals who could bridge the gap between the analytical and the creative. [38]Design thinking—an inherently artistic process—is what turns a functional piece of technology into a revolutionary product.[/38] I share the view that we are currently over-prioritizing rote memorization in science. [40]By integrating arts, we teach students how to visualize complex data and think 'outside the box.'[/40] [39]The artificial divide between 'two cultures' is a 20th-century relic that is hindering 21st-century progress.[/39]
texts.C: [38]I agree that the integration of arts can foster innovation, but I share the concern that the term 'STEAM' has become a buzzword used to justify budget cuts in pure arts programs.[/38] If the only way to save music or painting is to prove they help people build better robots, we have already lost the battle. [40]Arts should be valued for their own sake, not as a 'servant' to the sciences.[/40] However, [37]I disagree that the arts are 'less rigorous'; a high-level mastery of music or fine art requires as much discipline and practice as any calculus course.[/37]
texts.D: The debate over STEAM misses a crucial point: the labor market of the future will automate 'pure' technical skills first. [39]What cannot be easily automated is the empathy, ethical judgment, and narrative sense that the humanities and arts provide.[/39] While I share the skepticism that adding a bit of drawing to a physics class is a 'solution,' I believe a radical overhaul of our education system is needed. We shouldn't just add an 'A' to STEM; we should rebuild our curriculum around the 'human' skills that science alone cannot provide.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether the arts can be integrated with science without losing rigor?
questions.explanation: Expert A argues that arts 'dilute' the curriculum and distract from core competencies. Expert C argues that arts are not 'less rigorous' and require as much discipline as calculus, supporting their value in education.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the role of creativity in technological breakthroughs?
questions.explanation: Expert B says design thinking turns technology into 'revolutionary products.' Expert C agrees that 'integration of arts can foster innovation.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the limitations of a purely technical education for the future job market?
questions.explanation: Expert D says 'pure' technical skills will be automated and we need 'human' skills. Expert B also argues that the 'artificial divide' between cultures is hindering progress in the 21st century.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the primary reason for including arts in the curriculum?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes arts should be valued 'for their own sake' and not as a servant to science. Expert B argues for arts inclusion primarily because it improves scientific and technological results (visualization, thinking outside the box).
